hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Speaking Without a Script -- Holding the Floor and Staying Fluent
lesson.menu_desc: On a live client call, the questions go off-script and Ana cannot freeze: she holds the floor, stalls to buy a second, signposts her answer, rewords what she cannot find, and gets back on track when she drifts onto a tangent -- that's a great question, let me come back to that in a moment -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- Fluency Strategies
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 18 -- Fluency Strategies
lesson.grammar_point: fluency strategies (fillers, holding the floor, paraphrasing)

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to keep control of the conversation and your turn to speak
to slow down on purpose to buy time before you answer
the line of thinking you are following as you speak
to go back and correct or restate what you just said
to use small phrases that guide the listener through your answer
to say the same thing using different words
to gradually stop speaking without finishing your sentence
to talk on and on in an aimless, unfocused way

paras: Ana is a QA analyst on a fully remote team, and last week she joined a live client call to present the test results for a release. She had prepared slides and a clear script. But five minutes in, the client, Greg, started asking questions that were nowhere in her plan: about a bug she had not touched, about a deadline she did not own, about a feature from a different team. The call had gone off-script, and for a moment Ana felt her mind go blank. In the past, that was exactly when she froze. She would ramble, lose her train of thought, and let her voice trail off in the middle of a sentence.

This time was different, because Ana had learned to hold the floor. When Greg asked something unexpected, she did not panic and she did not rush. She stalled for a second to buy time, calmly saying, 'That's a great question, let me think for a moment.' That short phrase did two things: it kept her turn, and it gave her brain a second to catch up. Then she signposted her answer so Greg could follow her: 'There are two parts to this. First, the bug you mentioned, and second, the timeline.' When she could not find the exact technical word, she did not stop; she simply reworded the idea in plainer English until it landed.

Ana also learned when to steer the conversation back. Twice, Greg pulled her onto a tangent about an old system, and instead of following him down every path, she gently got back on track: 'I don't want to digress, so let me come back to the release.' Once, she realized she had given a wrong date, so she backtracked cleanly: 'Sorry, let me rephrase that. I said Tuesday, but the fix ships on Thursday.' None of this was scripted. It was improvised, in real time, under pressure. By the end of the call, Greg thanked her for being so clear. The secret was not perfect English or a perfect script. The secret was staying fluent: keeping the floor, buying time when she needed it, and always finding another way to say the thing.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, fluency and speaking without a script

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana memorizes a longer script so that no client question can ever surprise her again.
When a live call goes off-script, Ana stays fluent by holding the floor, stalling to buy time, signposting, rewording, and getting back on track instead of freezing.
The text explains how to write automated tests for a new software release.

When Ana cannot find the exact technical word, she stops the call and looks it up.
The opposite: 'she did not stop; she simply reworded the idea in plainer English until it landed'.

gapfill.text: Notice that fluency is not about knowing every word. It is a set of STRATEGIES. To BUY TIME, use a stalling phrase: 'That's a great question, let me think for a moment.' To HOLD THE FLOOR, keep your turn: 'If I can just finish this point...' To STAY CLEAR, signpost and reword: 'There are two parts to this. First..., second...' and 'In other words...'. These phrases keep you talking and in control, even before you have the perfect word.

bank.label: Useful phrases for speaking without a script
items: That's a great question -- let me think for a moment.
If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to that.
There are two parts to this. First..., and second...
In other words, what I mean is that the fix is low-risk.
Sorry, let me rephrase that -- I said Tuesday, but I meant Thursday.
I don't want to digress, so let me come back to the release.
Let me come back to that in a moment, if that's okay.
Give me one second to find the right word.

lf.title: Notice the fluency move
items: let me think for a moment
, then I'll come to that." (hold the floor)
, what I mean is the fix is low-risk." (reword to stay clear)

lf.text: Four moves that keep you fluent without a script. STALL to buy a second before you answer. HOLD THE FLOOR to keep your turn when someone jumps in. SIGNPOST so the listener can follow your structure. REWORD when the exact word will not come. You are not filling silence with 'um' -- you are staying in control with a purpose.

items.situation: A client asks a question you did not expect and your mind goes blank. Stall to buy a second without freezing.
tips: That's a great question -- let me think for a moment.
Give me one second to find the right way to explain this.

items.situation: A teammate keeps interrupting, but you need to finish your point. Hold the floor politely.
tips: If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to your question.
Let me finish this thought, and then I'll hand it over.

items.situation: Your answer has two separate parts and the client looks lost. Signpost so they can follow.
tips: There are two parts to this. First the bug, and second the timeline.
Let me take that in two steps, so it's easy to follow.

items.situation: You cannot remember the exact technical term mid-sentence. Reword instead of stopping.
tips: In other words, the change only affects the login screen.
What I mean is, it's a small, low-risk fix.

items.situation: The client pulls you onto an old, off-topic system. Get back on track without being rude.
tips: I don't want to digress, so let me come back to the release.
That's a separate topic -- can I come back to that at the end?

items.situation: You realize you just gave the wrong date. Backtrack cleanly and correct it.
tips: Sorry, let me rephrase that -- the fix ships on Thursday, not Tuesday.
Let me correct myself: the deadline is Thursday.

items: A client asks an off-script question. Stall to buy time, then signpost your answer in two parts. Speak from your notes.
A teammate interrupts you mid-answer. Politely hold the floor, finish your point, and then hand over.
With no notes, present one QA result and handle two surprise questions: reword when a word will not come, and get back on track if you drift onto a tangent.
You realize you gave the wrong number. Backtrack cleanly, correct it, and keep going without losing your train of thought.

answerkey.title: Reveal the fluency toolkit
list: Vocabulary: to hold the floor, to stall, train of thought, to backtrack, to digress, tangent, to elaborate, to reword, to improvise, to signpost, to trail off, to ramble.
Buy time: 'That's a great question, let me think for a moment.'
Hold the floor: 'If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to that.'
Signpost: 'There are two parts to this. First..., and second...'
Reword: 'In other words, what I mean is...'; backtrack: 'Sorry, let me rephrase that.'
Strategy: keep the floor, buy time when you need it, and always find another way to say the thing.
answerkey.note: Fluency is not perfect English. It is staying in control: keep the floor, buy a second, and reword until the idea lands.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, it's Greg, from the client side. I just wanted to leave you a quick note after this morning's call. Honestly, that was the clearest update we have had from your team in months, and I know I did not make it easy. I threw a few questions at you that were completely off-script, that bug from the other team, the old system, the timeline, and you handled all of them without missing a beat. What I noticed is that you never rushed. When I asked something unexpected, you took a second, you said let me think for a moment, and then you gave me a structured answer, first this, then that. At one point you even stopped and said, sorry, let me rephrase that, and corrected the date. That kind of honesty builds trust. So here is my one follow-up: could you send me a short written summary of the two parts you walked me through, the bug and the timeline? No rush, end of day is fine. And seriously, well done today. You were calm, you were clear, and you kept the whole thing on track. Thanks, Ana.

Hey Ana, it's Karen, your team lead. I sat in on your client call today, and I want to give you one quick piece of coaching, because your fluency has come a long way. The thing you did really well was hold the floor. When the conversation went off-script, you did not freeze and you did not ramble. You bought yourself a second with a simple phrase, and that is exactly the right move. Here is my tip for next time, and it is a small one. When you buy time, try to signpost even more clearly. Say something like, let me answer that in two parts, and then actually number them out loud, first, second. It helps the client follow you, and honestly, it helps you follow yourself, so you do not lose your train of thought. One more thing: it is completely fine to reword. If the exact technical word will not come, do not stop the call to hunt for it. Just say, in other words, and explain it in plain English. Nobody is grading your vocabulary; they want to understand the release. Keep doing what you are doing. You are speaking without a script now, and it shows. Let us catch up on Friday.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana handled the off-script question on the client call. First, she stalled for a second and said, that's a great question, let me think for a moment. Then she signposted her answer, telling Greg there were two parts to it. After that, she reworded the idea in plainer English when the exact term would not come. Next, when Greg pulled her onto a tangent, she gently got back on track and returned to the release. Finally, she noticed a wrong date, backtracked cleanly, and corrected it without losing her train of thought.
